Strategy Comparison  |  Factor Investing  |  Portfolio Optimization
Beyond Equal Weight: Nine Allocation Strategies Compared
Momentum · Risk Parity · Trend Following · Max Sharpe · Min Variance
NASDAQ · S&P 500 · Gold · CSI Dividend (A-Shares)  —  2005–2026  |  21.2 Years  |  9 Strategies
Executive Summary

We evaluate eight alternative portfolio allocation strategies against an equal-weight permanent portfolio baseline (25% each, annual rebalancing) across four global assets over 21.2 years. Max Sharpe optimization is the clear winner: CAGR 13.76% (+183bps vs. baseline), Sharpe ratio 0.88, maximum drawdown −38.6%, growing $1 to $15.47. Risk Parity (ERC) and Minimum Variance reduce volatility below 14% and drawdown below −38.5% while preserving near-baseline returns — suitable for risk-constrained mandates. Trend-following strategies underperform on monthly data due to signal lag and whipsaw costs. All returns are price-only; dividends and transaction costs excluded.

1
Strategy Theory & Construction
Equal Weight (Annual Rebal)
Baseline
Harry Browne's Permanent Portfolio: equal capital allocation across uncorrelated assets, rebalanced annually. No forecasts required. Annual rebalancing captures the mean-reversion premium by systematically buying underperformers.
\[w_i = \frac{1}{N} = 25\% \quad \forall\, i\]
Inverse Volatility
Risk-Based
Lower-volatility assets receive proportionally higher capital allocation. Derived from the low-volatility anomaly (Baker & Haugen, 2012). Equalizes absolute dollar-risk contribution without a full covariance matrix.
\[w_i = \frac{1/\hat{\sigma}_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{N} 1/\hat{\sigma}_j}\]
\(\hat{\sigma}_i = \text{12-month rolling annualised volatility}\)
Momentum Tilt
Momentum
Cross-sectional momentum (Jegadeesh & Titman, 1993): assets with stronger 12-month relative performance tend to continue outperforming. Rank-based weights overweight recent leaders; rebalanced monthly.
\[w_i \;\propto\; \text{rank}_i\!\left(R^{12m}\right)\]
\(\text{rank } 1 = \text{worst},\; 4 = \text{best};\quad \sum w_i = 1\)
Trend Following (SMA12)
Trend
Moving-average filter identifies bull/bear regimes. Assets above their 12-month SMA are held in equal weight; assets below are replaced with cash. Aims to avoid extended drawdowns by exiting during confirmed downtrends.
\[w_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{k} & \text{if } P_i > \overline{P}_i^{\,12m} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]
\(k = \text{number of assets currently in uptrend}\)
Dual Momentum
Momentum
Antonacci (2014): combines absolute momentum (asset vs. 0%) and relative momentum (cross-asset ranking). Only holds assets that pass both filters — positive 12-month return AND top relative rank.
\[w_i > 0 \iff R_i^{12m} > 0 \;\wedge\; \text{rank}_i = \text{top}\]
\(w_i \propto \text{rank}_i \cdot \mathbf{1}[R_i^{12m}>0];\quad \text{cash if no asset qualifies}\)
Risk Parity (ERC)
Risk-Based
Equal Risk Contribution (Maillard, Roncalli & Teiletche, 2010): each asset contributes equally to total portfolio variance. Solved iteratively via constrained optimisation using a rolling 36-month covariance matrix.
\[\frac{w_i\,(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w})_i}{\sigma_p} = \frac{w_j\,(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w})_j}{\sigma_p} \quad \forall\, i,j\]
\(\text{s.t. } \sum w_i = 1,\quad w_i \geq 0.05\)
Minimum Variance
Optimization
Markowitz (1952) efficient frontier left endpoint: minimise portfolio variance regardless of expected returns. Uses a 36-month rolling covariance matrix. No return forecast needed — tends to concentrate in low-vol, negatively-correlated assets.
\[\min_{\mathbf{w}}\; \mathbf{w}^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}\, \mathbf{w}\]
\(\text{s.t. } \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{1}=1,\quad 0.05 \leq w_i \leq 0.60\)
Max Sharpe (Tangency)
Optimization
Locate the tangency portfolio on the efficient frontier maximising the Sharpe ratio. Uses rolling 36-month estimates of expected returns and covariance. Solved monthly via SLSQP with bounded weights to limit concentration risk.
\[\max_{\mathbf{w}}\; \frac{\mathbf{w}^\top\boldsymbol{\mu} - r_f}{\sqrt{\mathbf{w}^\top\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\,\mathbf{w}}}\]
\(\text{s.t. } \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{1}=1,\quad 0.05 \leq w_i \leq 0.60\)
Momentum + Trend Combo
Combo
Dual-filter: requires both a positive trend (price > 10-month SMA) AND positive 12-month absolute momentum. Stricter entry reduces false signals but increases signal latency on monthly data.
\[\text{Signal}_i = \mathbf{1}\!\left[P_i > \overline{P}_i^{\,10m}\right] \cdot \mathbf{1}\!\left[R_i^{12m} > 0\right]\]
\(w_i = \frac{\text{Signal}_i}{\sum_j \text{Signal}_j};\quad \text{equal weight if } \sum_j \text{Signal}_j = 0\)

4
Rolling 3-Year Annualized Return
Exhibit 3. Rolling 36-month annualized return for each strategy. Max Sharpe shows consistently higher rolling returns across most regimes. Trend-following strategies show extreme swings — strong in momentum markets (2017–2020), weak during choppy regimes (2022).
5
Average Asset Allocation by Strategy
Exhibit 4. Time-averaged asset weights for each strategy. Darker = higher average allocation. Inverse Volatility and Min Variance overweight Gold and S&P 500 (lower vol assets). Max Sharpe dynamically tilts toward NASDAQ and Gold based on rolling return/covariance estimates.

Balance return & riskRisk Parity (ERC)11.85%0.81−38.5%Near-baseline return with significantly lower vol (13.7%) and smooth equity curve
Minimize drawdownInverse Volatility10.84%0.77−34.7%Lowest max drawdown of all strategies; simple, transparent, low turnover

Table 2. Strategy selection guide by investor objective.
Why Max Sharpe Wins

Rolling 36-month covariance estimation allows the optimizer to dynamically increase NASDAQ and Gold weights when their return-to-risk ratio is elevated, and reduce them during adverse regimes. The key constraint — bounding weights between 5% and 60% — prevents extreme concentration while still allowing meaningful tilts. Over 21 years, this dynamic tilting captures +183bps CAGR over static equal weight.

Why Trend Following Disappoints

Monthly data introduces a structural lag: by the time a 12-month SMA crossover occurs, most of the drawdown has already happened. The strategy then re-enters near the bottom of subsequent recovery rallies, creating persistent "whipsaw" patterns. Daily or weekly data would materially improve trend-following results — a limitation of the current monthly data infrastructure.

7
Conclusion
Investment Conclusion

For investors seeking to maximize risk-adjusted returns from this four-asset universe, Max Sharpe optimization with monthly rebalancing is the dominant strategy: CAGR 13.76%, Sharpe 0.88, $1 → $15.47 over 21.2 years. For risk-constrained mandates, Risk Parity (ERC) delivers near-baseline returns (11.85%) with materially lower volatility (13.7%) and smoother drawdown profile. The equal-weight baseline remains compelling for its simplicity and robustness to estimation error — no model is guaranteed to perform as well in the next 21 years as it did in the last 21.

Practical note: Max Sharpe requires monthly rebalancing and rolling covariance estimation — transaction costs (estimated 0.1–0.3% per rebalance) could reduce its realized advantage by ~30–60bps annually, narrowing but not eliminating the gap versus equal weight.

QUANT RESEARCH

IMPORTANT DISCLOSURES
Educational purposes only. Not investment advice. Past performance not indicative of future results. Price-return only; dividends, transaction costs, taxes, and currency effects excluded. Max Sharpe and Min Variance bounds: 5%–60% per asset. Risk Parity uses 36-month rolling covariance. Data: Yahoo Finance (^IXIC, ^GSPC, GC=F), Eastmoney (000922.SS), Sina Finance. Period: 2005-01 to 2026-04. Generated: April 22, 2026.
